analogy: Think of "Electromagnetic Waves" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter Eight ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES 8.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 4, we learnt that an electric current produces magnetic field and that two current-carrying wires exert a magnetic force on each other. Further, in Chapter 6, we have seen that a magnetic field changing with time gives... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Electromagnetic Waves is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that chapter Eight ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES 8.1 INTRODUCTION In Chapter 4, we learnt that an electric current produces magnetic field and that two current-carrying wires exert a magnetic force on each other.

The text explains that further, in Chapter 6, we have seen that a magnetic field changing with time gives rise to an electric field.

The text explains that does an electric field changing with time give rise to a magnetic field?

The text explains that james Clerk Maxwell (1831-1879), argued that this was indeed the case – not only an electric current but also a time-varying electric field generates magnetic field.

The text explains that while applying the Ampere’s circuital law to find magnetic field at a point outside a capacitor connected to a time-varying current, Maxwell noticed an inconsistency in the Ampere’s circuital law.

The text explains that he suggested the existence of an additional current, called by him, the displacement current to remove this inconsistency.

The text explains that maxwell formulated a set of equations involving electric and magnetic fields, and their sources, the charge and current densities.

The text explains that these equations are known as Maxwell’s equations.

The text explains that together with the Lorentz force formula (Chapter 4), they mathematically express all the basic laws of electromagnetism.

The text explains that the most important prediction to emerge from Maxwell’s equations is the existence of electromagnetic waves, which are (coupled) time- varying electric and magnetic fields that propagate in space.

The text explains that the speed of the waves, according to these equations, turned out to be very close to

The text explains that physics 202 the speed of light( 3 ×108 m/s), obtained from optical measurements.

The text explains that this led to the remarkable conclusion that light is an electromagnetic wave.

The text explains that maxwell’s work thus unified the domain of electricity, magnetism and light.

The text explains that hertz, in 1885, experimentally demonstrated the existence of electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that its technological use by Marconi and others led in due course to the revolution in communication that we are witnessing today.

The text explains that then we present a descriptive account of electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that the broad spectrum of electromagnetic waves, stretching from g rays (wavelength ~10–12 m) to long radio waves (wavelength ~106 m) is described.

The text explains that this effect is of great importance because it explains the existence of radio waves, gamma rays and visible light, as well as all other forms of electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that for this, we consider a plane circular loop of radius r whose plane is perpendicular to the direction of the current-carrying wire, and which is centred symmetrically with respect to the wire [Fig.

The text explains that from symmetry, the magnetic field is directed along the circumference of the circular loop and is the same in magnitude at all points on the loop so that if B is the magnitude of the field, the left side of Eq.

The text explains that from these he arrived at the most important conclusion that light is an electromagnetic wave.

The text explains that 203 Electromagnetic Waves Now, consider a different surface, which has the same boundary.

The text explains that the source of a magnetic field is not just the conduction electric current due to flowing FIGURE 8.1 A parallel plate capacitor C, as part of a circuit through which a time dependent current i (t) flows, (a) a loop of radius r, to determine magnetic field at a point P on the loop; (b) a pot-shaped surface passing through the interior between the capacitor plates with the loop shown in (a) as its rim; (c) a tiffin- shaped surface with the circular loop as its rim and a flat circular bottom S between the capacitor plates.

The text explains that in such a region, we expect a magnetic field, though there is no (conduction) current source nearby!

The text explains that (a). * They are still not perfectly symmetrical; there are no known sources of magnetic field (magnetic monopoles) analogous to electric charges which are sources of electric field.

The text explains that 205 Electromagnetic Waves a consequence of the displacement current being a source of a magnetic field.

The text explains that one very important consequence of this symmetry is the existence of electromagnetic waves, which we discuss qualitatively in the next section.

The text explains that mAXWELL’S EQUATIONS IN VACUUM 1. “E.dA = Q/✒0 (Gauss’s Law for electricity) 2. “B.dA = 0 (Gauss’s Law for magnetism) 3. “E.dl = –d d B Φ t l = (Faraday’s Law) 4. “B.dl = = d d 0 μ μ ε 0 0 i t c E + Φ (Ampere – Maxwell Law) 8.3 ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES 8.3.1 Sources of electromagnetic waves How are electromagnetic waves produced?

The text explains that neither stationary charges nor charges in uniform motion (steady currents) can be sources of electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that it is an important result of Maxwell’s theory that accelerated charges radiate electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that (An oscillating charge is an example of accelerating charge.) This produces an oscillating electric field in space, which produces an oscillating magnetic field, which in turn, is a source of oscillating electric field, and so on.

The text explains that the oscillating electric and magnetic fields thus regenerate each other, so to speak, as the wave propagates through the space.

The text explains that the frequency of the electromagnetic wave naturally equals the frequency of oscillation of the charge.

The text explains that the energy associated with the propagating wave comes at the expense of the energy of the source – the accelerated charge.

The text explains that from the preceding discussion, it might appear easy to test the prediction that light is an electromagnetic wave.

The text explains that physics 206 wave had to come in the low frequency region (the radio wave region), as in the Hertz’s experiment (1887).

The text explains that seven years after Hertz, Jagdish Chandra Bose, working at Calcutta (now Kolkata), succeeded in producing and observing electromagnetic waves of much shorter wavelength (25 mm to 5 mm).

The text explains that at around the same time, Guglielmo Marconi in Italy followed Hertz’s work and succeeded in transmitting electromagnetic waves over distances of many kilometres.

The text explains that marconi’s experiment marks the beginning of the field of communication using electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that 8.3.2 Nature of electromagnetic waves It can be shown from Maxwell’s equations that electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are perpendicular to each other, and to the direction of propagation.

The text explains that the electric field inside the plates of the capacitor is directed perpendicular to the plates.

The text explains that 8.3, we show a typical example of a plane electromagnetic wave propagating along the z direction (the fields are shown as a function of the z coordinate, at a given time t).

The text explains that the electric and magnetic fields Ex and By are perpendicular to each other, and to the direction z of propagation.

The text explains that we can write Ex and By as follows: Ex= E0 sin (kz– wt ) [8.7(a)] By= B0 sin (kz– wt ) [8.7(b)] Here k is related to the wave length l of the wave by the usual equation 2 k λ π = (8.8) EXAMPLE 8.1 Heinrich Rudolf Hertz (1857 – 1894) German physicist who was the first to broadcast and receive radio waves.

The text explains that he produced electro- magnetic waves, sent them through space, and measured their wave- length and speed.

The text explains that what is the frequency of the electromagnetic waves produced by the oscillator?

The text explains that 8.7 The amplitude of the magnetic field part of a harmonic electromagnetic wave in vacuum is B0 = 510 nT.

The text explains that what is the amplitude of the electric field part of the wave?

The text explains that 8.8 Suppose that the electric field amplitude of an electromagnetic wave is E0 = 120 N/C and that its frequency is n = 50.0 MHz.

The text explains that 8.9 The terminology of different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum is given in the text.

The text explains that use the formula E = h n (for energy of a quantum of radiation: photon) and obtain the photon energy in units of eV for different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8: Electromagnetic Waves

prelims_traps: Do not treat ELECTROMAGNETIC and WAVES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Electromagnetic Waves; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Electromagnetic Waves through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use ELECTROMAGNETIC and WAVES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
